coredata.dc:title: Blue–green luminescent carbon nanodots produced in a silica matrix
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Brito, Jackeline Barbosa
Costa, Tania Maria Haas
Rodembusch, Fabiano Severo
Konowalow, Anthony Silveira
dos Reis, Roberto Moreno Souza
Balzaretti, Naira Maria
da Jornada, João Alziro Herz
coredata.dc:description: Blue–green luminescent carbon nanodots entrapped in silica matrix were produced by the pyrolysis of methyl groups well dispersed on the edges of nanometric grains of silica (Aerosil® R974) previously cold sintered under high pressure. Selected Area Electron Diffraction and Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy analyses confirmed the formation of carbon nanodots with graphitic sp2 bonds, without additional passivation technique. Photoluminescence spectra were dependent on the excitation wavelength and the higher emission intensity was observed in the range between 450 and 500nm for the sample cold sintered at 7.7GPa and thermal treated at 800°C, corresponding to a total fluorescence quantum yield of about 11%.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and chemical characterization of the C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of C-dots preparation based on Aerosil® R974. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Top panel: images of monolithic samples cold sintered at 2.5, 4.0 and 7.7GPa (left) and after heating at 800°C (right). Bottom panel: images of the corresponding samples heated at 800°C after cold sintering at 2.5, 4 and 7.7GPa, respectively. The scale is in mm. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM micrograph of C-dots produced from Aerosil® R974 processed at 7.7GPa and heat-treated at 800°C, after removing the silica matrix by HF etching. The corresponding size distribution of the C-dots is also presented (right). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SAED pattern from C-dots with the respective lattice plane index (left). High magnification micrograph containing one large C-dot (right). A CBED pattern from the circular red area is also shown (inset). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
EELS spectrum from C-dots showing the transitions edges from carbons sp2 (π∗) and sp3 (σ∗) at 285eV and 290eV, respectively.

FTIR spectra of Aerosil© R974 in the range of CH n absorption bands for the samples cold sintered at 4GPa (left) and 7.7GPa (right): (a) pristine, (b) cold sintered at high pressure, and (c) pyrolysis at 800°C after cold sintering at high pressure.

Photoluminescence spectra for distinct excitation wavelengths from Aerosil© R974 after cold sintering at 7.7GPa followed by a thermal treatment at 500°C (left) and 800°C (right). The absorbance spectra of the samples heated at 500°C and 800°C are also shown (inset). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Blue–green luminescent carbon nanodots produced in a silica matrix

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue–green luminescent carbon nanodots entrapped in silica matrix were produced by the pyrolysis of methyl groups well dispersed on the edges of nanometric grains of silica (Aerosil® R974) previously cold sintered under high pressure. Selected Area Electron Diffraction and Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy analyses confirmed the formation of carbon nanodots with graphitic sp2 bonds, without additional passivation technique. Photoluminescence spectra were dependent on the excitation wavelength and the higher emission intensity was observed in the range between 450 and 500nm for the sample cold sintered at 7.7GPa and thermal treated at 800°C, corresponding to a total fluorescence quantum yield of about 11%.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon nanodots (C-dots) are emergent materials that have been intensively studied in the last few years due to their large efficiency in fluorescence emission, biocompatibility, low cost and low toxicity [1,2]. Usually these materials present high oxygen content and show a broad photoluminescence (PL) emission strongly dependent on the excitation wavelength. C-dots can be synthesized either by top-down or by bottom-up procedures. In the top-down approach, the C-dots have been produced, for instance, by arc discharge [3], laser ablation [4–9] or electrochemical oxidation [10–13] from a larger carbon structure. On the other hand, in the bottom-up procedures, C-dots have been produced from molecular precursors using thermal or chemical activated methods [14–20]. In both cases, the surfaces of the C-dots are passivated by oxygen and/or nitrogen and require purification/separation techniques, such as centrifugation, dialysis or electrophoresis [21,22]. The use of different surface passivation agents makes it possible to increase the solubility of C-dots in non-aqueous solvents and to modify the PL properties [1]. The typical size of these nanostructures is in the range between 1 and 4nm. The structure of the C-dots has been investigated by Raman spectroscopy, 13C Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (13C NMR), Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The compilation of the available results indicates that C-dots mainly consist of sp2 carbon forming an amorphous to nanocrystalline core, where the lattice spacing is consistent with graphitic or turbostratic carbon [1]. Additionally, C-dots present carboxylic groups at the surface with oxygen content ranging from 5 to 50wt%, which can be useful for further transformations [1]. Concerning the photo physical properties of C-dots, these materials show intense UV absorption bands starting at ∼270nm. The emissive properties indicate a broad color range of PL tailored by the excitation wavelength. The origin of the PL emission of C-dots is still not completely understood but it is probably related to the quantum effect due to the confinement in the nanoscale or to the emissive traps attached to the surface of the C-dots [1,23].

In a previous work [24] nanometric graphitic structures were produced through the pyrolysis under high pressure of methyl groups dispersed at the surface of nanometric particles of silica. The resulting nanocomposite consisted of silica sintered at high pressure and high temperature containing less than 3wt% of well dispersed carbon nanostructures. The samples looked glassy and black. In this work, luminescent blue–green C-dots were produced embedded in a transparent silica glass previously cold sintered at 7.7GPa. The difference compared to the previous work was that the pyrolysis of the methyl groups dispersed on the edges of nanometric grains of silica (Aerosil® R974) was, now, done at ambient pressure, after cold sintering the silica matrix under high pressure. The resulting nanocomposites still looked glassy but transparent and yellowish, instead of black. These luminescent nanocomposites should be very suitable for lighting devices since they require excitation in the visible range and they are enclosed in a silica casing, which is environmentally friendly and presents good mechanical and thermal properties.

Hydrophobic Aerosil© R974 (Evonik, Inc) was used as raw material to produce the C-dots and consists of silica grains with average particle size of 12nm containing methyl terminal groups at the surface (0.7–1.3wt% of carbon content). Initially, the pristine silica powder (Aerosil© R974) (white powder) was compacted in a piston-cylinder type die at ∼0.25GPa into cylinders of 3.0mm diameter and 2.6mm height. The cylinders were, then, individually placed inside a capsule made of hBN surrounded by a ceramic gasket and pressed at 2.5, 4.0 and 7.7GPa at room temperature during 2min using a toroidal high pressure apparatus. Details about the experimental setup are presented elsewhere [24]. After that, the cold sintered monolithic samples were removed from the hBN capsule and submitted to a thermal treatment from 300 to 800°C under Ar flux (heating rate of 10°Cmin− 1) at atmospheric pressure, to promote the pyrolysis of the methyl terminal groups to form the C-dots in the silica matrix. Fig. 1 illustrates the steps of preparing C-dots proposed in this work.

Thermogravimetric analysis of the Aerosil samples was done in a Shimadzu TGA50 analyzer from room temperature to 800°C at a rate of 10°Cmin−1 in Ar atmosphere to determine the temperature range correspondent to the modification of the methyl groups. The absorption spectra of the monolithic samples of silica containing the C-dots were measured with a Shimadzu UV2450PC spectrophotometer using an ISR-2200 Integrating Sphere Attachment. The experiments were performed at room temperature and the baseline in the solid-state was obtained using BaSO4 (Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Ltd.). The PL emission spectra of the monolithic samples were measured with a Shimadzu RF5301PC spectrofluorometer. Sodium salicylate (Φ fl =0.42) was used as quantum yield standard in the solid state [25].

Transmission Electron Microscopy was done with Zeiss® Libra 120kV equipment operating at 80kV for morphological and chemical characterization of the carbon nanostructures. For this analysis, samples were immersed in a HF solution to remove the SiO2 matrix. After that, the solution was rinsed in isopropyl alcohol and dropped on a carbon coated copper grid. Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS) and Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) analyses were also carried out in the same microscope to investigate the chemical nature of the synthesized particles.
A Bomem MB 100 FTIR spectrometer with a resolution of 4cm−1 (with 32 scans) was used to investigate the CH n content in the nanocomposites after cold sintering and thermal treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and chemical characterization of the C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the monolithic samples after cold sintering under high pressure followed by thermal treatment. It is worth mentioning that after cold sintering, all samples were transparent, colorless. After heating at 800°C the color of the monoliths was dependent of the value of pressure used for cold sintering: the samples sintered at 2.5GPa remained transparent and colorless while the samples sintered at 4GPa and 7.7GPa became light and dark yellow, respectively, under illumination of fluorescent lamp. It is worth mentioning here that, in the previous work [24], where the pyrolysis was done in situ, under high pressure, the monolithic samples turned black in the pressure range from 2.5 to 7.7GPa, due to the formation of nanographitic structures. In this work, where the pyrolysis was done at ambient pressure after cold sintering under high pressure, the samples became yellow and this effect was related to the formation of C-dots, as it will be discussed below. It is also important to remark that, when the pyrolysis was done without previous cold sintering under high pressure, the sample remained white and all the CH n content was removed above 600°C, as indicated by FTIR and TGA analyses.

Fig. 3 presents a TEM micrograph of the Aerosil© R974 cold sintered at 7.7GPa and heated at 800°C, after removing the silica matrix by HF etching, showing well-dispersed round-like type C-dots. For this particular micrograph, 404 particles were counted corresponding to an average size of (6.8±1.2) nm. Noteworthy the size distribution was not uniform among several TEM images taken from different spots of the sample, comprising particles from 1–2nm up to 40–50nm. This distribution can probably be related to the high pressure sintering and diffusive processes occurring in different regions of the silica matrix, where it should be expected that the conditions close to the center of the silica matrix would be slightly different compared to the conditions near the surface during the pyrolysis. This heterogeneous size distribution was also observed by TEM for samples processed at 500 and 700°C and, therefore, it seemed to make no sense the assignment of an average grain size for these samples.

The SAED pattern taken from a collection of C-dots within a 200nm diameter circular aperture demonstrated the crystalline nature of the synthesized C-dots (Fig. 4 ). Measurements of the interplanar distances were in agreement with the theoretical values for the hexagonal graphitic structure concerning the P 63/mmc space group (2.094Å, 1.762Å and 1.526Å). These values are related to the (100), (102) and (103) planes of graphite. The inset presents the convergent beam electron diffraction (CBED) taken from a large C-dot revealing its graphitic crystalline nature.

The EELS spectrum was taken from a region with heavy density of C-dots focused on the carbon K-edge absorption (Fig. 5 ). The peak located at 285eV can be ascribed to 1s-π∗ transitions due to sp2 bonded carbon, confirming the chemical nature do the C-dots [26]. It can also be observed the edge due to σ∗ states absorption located at 290eV.

Fig. 6 presents the FTIR spectra of the monolithic samples containing C-dots, focused on the region between 2600 and 3300cm−1 in order to investigate the absorption bands related to CH n . The original spectra were subtracted from a baseline using the software Origin© 8.0. The full FTIR spectra are presented as Supplementary data.

The spectrum of pristine Aerosil© R974 presents, as expected, two absorption bands related to the asymmetrical and symmetrical C–H stretching, located at 2970 and 2910cm−1, respectively. Cold sintering at 4.0GPa and 7.7GPa induced a drastic decrease in the intensity of the CH n absorption bands as well as, the appearance of new bands. After pyrolysis, the intensities remained small showing some changes compared to the spectra after cold sintering. The new bands located at 3070 and 3028cm−1 can be related to asymmetrical sp2-like carbons, which would be related to the beginning of the C-dots formation. It is worth mentioning that a significant amount of sp3-like CH n remained entrapped inside the silica matrix even after heat treatment at 800°C, indicating that after the C-dots formation residual sp3-like was still present in the silica matrix.

Results from thermal analyses of pristine Aerosil© R974 and for the monolithic samples after cold sintering are presented as Supplementary data. The weight loss due to the organic content of the silica matrix and due to the dehydroxylation reactions of bridged silanol groups can be observed from 200 to 600°C. The weight loss was larger for the cold sintered samples due to the dehydroxylation of the silica matrix induced at high pressure as a consequence of the stronger hydrogen bond interactions [27].

Fig. 7 shows the PL spectra using different excitation wavelengths for monolithic samples sintered at 7.7GPa and heated at 500°C (left) and 800°C (right). The inset of this figure presents the respective absorption spectra. The observed absorption profile is very similar to the ones reported for C-dots, presenting a strong absorption in the UV region (230–320nm) and a large FWHM of about 98nm, with a tail in the visible range [1,2,26,28,29].

It is important to remark that all the experiments were conducted at the same heating rate (10°C/min) and the same duration time at maximum temperature (10min). For temperatures lower than 500°C, the PL intensity was low, as should be expected since the thermal decomposition of the methyl groups would happen between 200 and 600°C according to the results from thermal analysis. In fact, the low intensity of the PL spectra at 500°C would be related to the incomplete pyrolysis of the methyl groups. For temperatures higher than 800°C the samples became black, probably due to the increase of the size of the graphitic structures induced by diffusion. In addition, a possible influence of the PL of hBN residues from the capsule used for processing at high pressure was discarded since the absorption and emission of hBN in the visible range are negligible [30].

It is worth mentioning that the silica matrix sintered under high pressure before the thermal treatment presented PL in the UV-Vis region (Fig. S7 – Supplementary data), which was not observed for the pristine powder of the precursor Aerosil© R974. The higher intensities were observed under excitation at 360–400nm, which can be related to short-range structures and defects in the silica structures [31–33]. The contribution of the silica matrix can clearly be observed around 350nm under excitation wavelengths 220–300nm from Aerosil© R974 after cold sintering at 7.7GPa followed by a thermal treatment at 500°C.

As already discussed in the literature, the most particular photophysical behavior of the C-dots is related to the dependence on the excitation wavelength of the intensity and maxima location of the PL spectra. This behavior can be ascribed to a wide size distribution of C-dots or even to emissive traps at the surface of the C-dots [1,2,34]. The wide PL bandwidth may be related to the chemical structure or to diverse PL centers [2]. The samples cold sintered at 7.7GPa after heat treatment at 500°C presented almost the same PL intensity and a small dependence on the excitation wavelength, showing a narrow color range with emission maxima located in the range 450–500nm. On the other hand the intensity of the PL spectra of the samples annealed at 800°C depend on the excitation wavelength, although the intensity maxima are also in the range 450–500nm. Since the intensity and maxima location dependence can be ascribed to a size effect of the C-dot, the PL spectra indicates that the nanodots prepared using the pressure/temperature treatment seems to have a narrow size distribution. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) in the solid state was calculated taking into account the total fluorescence emission of the nanocomposite, considering the contribution of the sintered silica matrix before the thermal treatment (Fig. S7). Values in the range from 2% to 9% were obtained for samples annealed at 500°C and in the range from 2% to 11% for samples annealed at 800°C, depending on the excitation wavelength (Table S1). It is worth mentioning that higher values for the QY for samples annealed at 500°C were obtained using an excitation range of 260–300nm, probably due to the contribution of the silica matrix and to the incomplete pyrolysis of the methyl groups. In contrast, samples annealed at 800°C presented higher QY under excitation in the visible range 440–460nm, probably due to the C-dots.

It is expected that the high pressure sintering at room temperature would promote chemical interactions among the surfaces of the silica nanograins [27], which allows the formation of a stiff structure with closed pores containing dispersed CH n groups. It should also be expected that increasing the pressure the interaction between the grains surfaces would be more effective, stiffening the matrix. Thus, the carbon nanoclusters formation during the thermal treatment would be related to chemical rearrangements at the surface of the silica nanograins. Kober et al. [35] and Hoffmann et al. [36] already pointed out that cold sintering of silica under high pressure is a very efficient and alternative method to produce stiff matrix with closed pores which is resistant to leaching and suitable for dye encapsulation. In this way, the dependence of the PL spectra on the annealing temperature should also be related to the diffusion of the carbon atoms required to form de C-dots during the pyrolysis of the CH n groups encapsulated inside these closed pores of the silica matrix. Higher temperatures would help the formation of C-dots.

Blue–green luminescent C-dots entrapped in transparent silica was produced by the direct pyrolysis of methyl groups present in nanometric silica grains (Aerosil® R974) previously cold sintered under high pressure. The intensity of the PL spectra was dependent on the excitation wavelength and the higher fluorescence quantum yield was observed in the blue–green region for the sample annealed at 800°C for excitation at 440–460nm. The SAED and EELS analyses confirmed the presence of sp2 carbon in the matrix, related to the C-dots formation with size close to 6–7nm. The effect of high pressure cold sintering would promote the formation of a stiff structure of silica with closed pores containing methyl groups. No passivation technique was required since the silica matrix itself would avoid the quenching of the luminescent properties. It is expected that the luminescent nanocomposites would also have excellent mechanical properties, besides good thermal and chemical stability, due to the well-known mechanical and thermal properties of sintered silica matrix. These properties may be very suitable for lighting devices. On the other hand, it is also possible to isolate the C-dots simply by removing silica by HF etching.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank CNPq/FAPERGS (Pronex), and CAPES for the financial support and to Evonik, Inc for gently supplying the Aerosil© R974 samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.04.062.
